It's a great puzzle game. It's difficult, so be patient.

If architecture school was this easy, we’d all be engineers with degrees printed on napkins. My bridges collapsed faster than my patience. Every bridge I built turned into modern art the second a truck touched it. Critics call it 'avant garde.' I call it uninstalling.

There are a lot of levels in this game, but none of them are different. They are all the same. The game has a very limited amount of features and materials to build with, so it gets boring very quickly. I legit feel like I wasted my money buying this game. It's awful.

I am a 45 yo father, probably one of the oldest people playing this game. I am a single father to my Son, who is 14 now. My son got this game for Christmas in 2021 from his uncle, so we installed it on his computer and he started playing. By the end of the week he had 24 hours on this game. This was horrible for me, as it was already hard for me to find ways to spend time with my son, as he is always out with his friends or just watching YouTube. So i decided to make a Steam account and get this game to see if I could maybe play alongside him. I loaded into the game, picked my character and world and started playing but I was stuck on what you where supposed to do. I asked my Son for help and he hosted a game for me to join. I loved it as it was the best time I had spent with my Son since my wife had died. This game has ever since brought me and my son closer again and now we actually spend time together outside the house together as well. This game reminded me that there's fun to be had in everything, and it has brought both me and my Son many happy memories.
RIP Francis Scott Key.

Bridge Constructor is a delightful blend of engineering puzzles and creativity that challenges your problem-solving skills in the most entertaining way possible. It's a game that manages to make the often daunting task of building bridges not only accessible but genuinely fun.

With its intuitive controls and a variety of materials at your disposal, the game provides a sandbox where imagination meets engineering. Designing and constructing bridges becomes an addictive endeavor as you strive to create sturdy structures capable of withstanding the laws of physics and the weight of traffic.

The gradual introduction of new mechanics keeps the gameplay fresh and engaging. From simple wooden bridges to complex suspension structures, each level presents a new challenge that will test your understanding of physics and construction principles.

Watching your vehicles traverse the bridges you've meticulously crafted is both nerve-wracking and immensely satisfying. Bridge Constructor offers a perfect balance between learning and enjoyment, making it an excellent choice for both casual players and engineering enthusiasts.

If you're looking for a game that exercises your brain while providing a fun and rewarding experience, Bridge Constructor is the gateway to a world of creative engineering challenges
Love it
Thompson
Classic, great!
dumb bridges not nice bridges 0/10

Bridge Constructor is a gem of a game that seamlessly blends creativity with engineering prowess, delivering an addictive and enjoyable experience for both casual gamers and bridge-building enthusiasts alike.

The game's premise is simple yet brilliant: construct sturdy bridges to facilitate the safe passage of vehicles across various terrains and challenges. What starts as a seemingly straightforward task evolves into a delightful puzzle-solving adventure, where each level presents unique obstacles that demand strategic thinking and creative construction.

One of the game's standout features is its intuitive and user-friendly interface, making the process of building and testing bridges a breeze. The learning curve is expertly crafted, allowing players to grasp the basics quickly before diving into more complex and challenging scenarios. The gradual introduction of new materials and construction elements keeps the gameplay fresh and engaging throughout.

The game's physics engine deserves special commendation. It adds a layer of realism that makes every bridge collapse or success feel like a genuine achievement. Watching your carefully crafted structures withstand the stress of moving vehicles or, on occasion, succumb to the forces of nature is both thrilling and educational.

Bridge Constructor's blend of education and entertainment is commendable. It not only entertains players with its clever puzzles but also imparts a basic understanding of structural engineering principles. This educational aspect is seamlessly integrated into the gameplay, making the experience both intellectually stimulating and enjoyable.

With a plethora of levels, diverse environments, and a sandbox mode for endless creativity, Bridge Constructor offers a fantastic value for its price. The game's charming visuals and lighthearted approach add an extra layer of enjoyment, making it a title that can be enjoyed by players of all ages.

In conclusion, Bridge Constructor is a triumph in the puzzle and simulation genre. It strikes a perfect balance between challenge and fun, making it a must-play for anyone with an inclination toward construction and engineering or those seeking a uniquely satisfying gaming experience. Building bridges has never been this entertaining!
played some bridgeconstructror games, this is the worst

As a bridge constructor it's okay, it does what it needs to.

My main problem is whenever a game forces you to complete a game on X difficulty to unlock XX difficulty, then having to fully replay the game in the XX difficulty to acquire further unlocks (or in this case achievements).

Dark pattern behaviour to force more playtime unnecessarily.

Completionists beware. Average gamers enjoy.

The game falls down pretty badly on it's simulation of "real-life". The math behind the game is flawed and over simplified. Also, you are constantly limited by money and materials, which heavily stifles any design flair you might want to express. Complex and interesting designs are out of the question, because you can’t afford them. All the time you are reduced to the most basic designs. Add to that the fact that there isn’t a analysis mode that allows you to understand the forces on your bridge, and you end up with a rather basic game, that can be somewhat limited. 
Having said that, it's still fun and certainly about the best one of this type you can get. I just wish they had a "sandbox" mode.  
So, recommended? When it's on sale, yes, it is fun, just don't expect it to be too real-life, and don't for heavens sake pay full price for it!

UI is awful and clunky.

I bought this at 90% off, and there is a reason Steam was willing to put it so low. It is not worth $10, and it's barely worth the dollar I spent on it. Just get Poly Bridge.

[h1]Overview:[/h1]
'[i]Bridge Constructor[/i]' is one of many construction games where the player must build a bridge sturdy enough to carry a variety of vehicles from one side to the other while keeping within budget and without the bridge breaking. You play as a bridge engineer as you build your way through 5 islands all of which are in desperate need of bridges because for some reason roads are paved but blueprints for bridges are left unaddressed. You go from building simple and straightforward bridges to gigantic monstrosities that more or less resemble roller coasters than an actual bridge. Although the idea sounds fun enough, it soon becomes more stressful and frustrating than fun and amusing and that is due to a multitude of reasons.

[h1]Gameplay:[/h1]
Honestly these types of games aren't my cup of tea so take this review with a grain of salt. It might not be as bad as I portray it to be as I myself am not a physics major but I think many agree that the physics in the game alone are inconsistent and not very good. Although it didn't take me extremely long to understand how the mechanics worked, it still didn't change the fact that the things you have to build in this game are absurdly ridiculous. The window of constructing bridges in later levels become so small and tight that it feels like there are only one or two possible solutions and that has a lot to do with the budget feature in the game. It's like every island you're contracted to had budget cuts so severe that I could've sworn I heard the board talking about implementing loot boxes to their marketing campaign to increase revenue.

I can understand giving players a challenge by limiting the resources they have to build a bridge, but the bar they set for money conservation is so high that you practically need to make the most flimsy and extremely brittle overpasses that coast on the verge of breaking just because the city said "[i][b]Hey, that's too much money for a bridge that'll keep our citizens safe! Take this instead![/b][/i]". The foundations and materials also range from wood, metal, stone pillars and wires and depending on what location and level you're playing, you're only given specific ones. So at the end of the day, you're not only limited to a strict budget, but also the resources the game provides you with. You don't get to unlock better materials, different foundations or anything that could give the player creative freedom. It's just what the game says you can use which limits potential solutions even more.

Had there been a casual mode where annoying budgets were nonexistent, I feel like the game might actually be fun. But as is, the limited resources the player's given as a whole makes the entire game a frustrating trek because the city just doesn't give a damn how dangerous a bridge is, as long as the vehicles testing it make it across. They don't care if it's next to impossible to build a bridge with a $20,000 budget, just make it happen! I at one point even had a bridge tested and break just to reset it and have it somehow hold together proving that the physics don't even follow their own rules. It becomes less about "[i][b]what awesome bridge can I build?[/b][/i]" and more about "[i][b]what the fuck actually works?[/b][/i]". It's just a game that tries to be like more popular bridge constructor games but fails to do so on more than one account.

[h1]Controls:[/h1]
Besides placing anchors on a grid, there's not much else to talk about in regards to controls as most of it is just observing the finished product.

[h1]Graphics:[/h1]
Not bad but not great either. Resembles other typical bridge constructor games and looks [i]very[/i] mobile-like. That may or may not be a bad thing depending on preference but personally I'm not a fan of them. Unlike the tanker, cars and trucks behave like toys, there's no damage or anything when they fail a bridge test. As for the trailer, that's probably the most fun because when it falls, it explodes! Nevertheless, the graphics are very underwhelming and just makes you feel like you're playing a mobile game.

[h1]Audio:[/h1]
Also not great. The sounds and music work fine enough but nothing stands out or sounds particularly great. The sound effects consist of below average vehicle sounds (some to which abruptly cut off) along with the annoying sound of bridges collapsing that you'll very soon come to hate. The music also gets repetitive and annoying fast but thank god there's a way to mute it because I need peace and quiet while I build the world's ugliest, flimsiest and most dangerous bridge with as little money as possible.

[h1]Conclusion/TL;DR:[/h1]
'[i]Bridge Constructor[/i]' is a physics-based puzzle game with a huge learning curve where you build bridges with specific materials while on a strict budget. The concept has been done more times than needed but the game itself is unfortunately one of the black sheeps of the flock. The budget feature is the main killer for many players (myself included) and cripple almost every potential (and safe) solution most levels could possibly have because the maximum amount of money you're allowed to spend is unrealistically low. This means only very few bridge options work and all fly dangerously close to succumbing which makes some levels almost impossible to beat. It also praises itself for having a cool physics engine but even the engine is inconsistent as some bridges will fail one time but pass the second. Overall, '[i]Bridge Constructor[/i]' tries to be like many of it's brothers and sisters but ended up being the ugly duckling of bridge constructors.

This game might be fun to tinker with but it ignores simple engineering physics.  I'm a civil engineer and this game refuses to acknowledge load physics.  It will not equally distribute load across members in certain ways and will instead load one member at a time until each fails.

[b]TLDR[/b] A traditional bridge construction game, didn't age particularly well. [11/20⭐, 21h🏅, 4/5💡]

[b]Context[/b] Bridge Constructor is traditional bridge construction game, currently including several free DLCs that largely improve playtime. Unfortunately, it didn’t age particularly well, but at least it made me appreciate [url=https://steamcommunity.com/id/macwh/recommended/684410/]Bridge Constructor Portal[/url] much more.

[b]Writing[/b] You’re a bridge builder and have to build bridges, that’s it.

[b]Aesthetics[/b] The visuals are simple without anything really standing out. (I suffered from a bug where fog turned opaque pink, which seems to be known for a long time). The soundtrack is pretty lousy and irritating, I had to turn it off a few levels into the game.

[b]Gameplay[/b] This is as traditional as a bridge construction game can get. You have to build bridges to make vehicles reach the other side of a cliff, with different anchor points to fix the structure. There are a few different materials, with different costs, and each level has a limited budget.

Personally, I found the levels in the main game to be too repetitive (this improved in the DLCs with slopping bridges) and the budget too restrictive for creative solutions (Bridge Constructor Portal registered the budget for scores but without limiting the player).

The levels are not particularly difficult with the regular vehicles, but the heavier ones can get pretty challenging. But this is mostly because the physics are so flimsy and the controls so rough that it provides for a frustrating experience of fine-tuning. Minimal changes can have unpredictable impact, and execution is not even deterministic (subsequent tries can have different results).

The interface itself is very rudimentary. It’s not possible to select which edges are roads, you can only move a point at a time, there is no option to speed up runs, it’s difficult to delete the expected piece, clicking the menu options places pieces. (Most of these have improved in Bridge Constructor Portal).

You can always restart a level and undo. Progress is auto-saved and the state of each bridge is saved.

[b]Value[/b] It took me 21 hours to beat the ~90 levels of all free expansions with all kinds vehicles, so the price seems adequate. You can always revisit previous levels, and there are multiple slots to save solutions. There’s no support for user levels. You only unlock the tank truck after finishing the levels with other vehicles, so you need to beat all levels twice. Achievements are mostly connected to passing the levels with different transport types, with a few exceptions that are easily achievable along the game (except for “Storm Warning” that I still don’t understand how I triggered it). Replay value tied to optimizing the cost of the bridges, if you’re into that kind of thing.
